title: Calculates EGFR values based on different types of formulas — egfr
text: scr vector Serum creatinine values mg/dL sex vector SEX values (female defined value 1) age vector AGE values years race vector RACE values (caucasian defined 1, black  Japanese > 2) ht vector HEIGHT values cm bun vector Blood urea nitrogen mg/dL scys vector Serum cystatin C mg/L prem vector PREM (premature) values (PREM defined value 1) bsa vector BSA values m2 provide case correction applied (see details) formula character formula used EGFR calculations (see details)

title: Calculates EGFR values based on different types of formulas — egfr
text: vector EGFR values

title: Calculates EGFR values based on different types of formulas — egfr
text: Currently  different formulas available calculations: "CKD-EPI": EGFR according Chronic Kidney Disease Epidemiology (CKD-EPI) study formula (Levey): $$ \textrm{eGFR} = 141 \cdot \min\left(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa},1\right)^{\alpha} \cdot \max\left(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa},1\right)^{-1.209} \cdot 0.993^{\textrm{Age}} \cdot 1.159 \textrm{ [black]} \cdot 1.018 \textrm{ [female]}$$ \(\min\left(\right)\) indicates minimum \(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa}\) 1; \(\max\left(\right)\) indicates maximum \(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa}\)1. scaling parameter \(\kappa\) 0.9 males 0.7 females scaling parameter \(\alpha\) -0.411 males -0.329 females. "CKD-EPI-ignore-race": EGFR according Chronic Kidney Disease Epidemiology (CKD-EPI) refit without race study formula (Delgado): $$ \textrm{eGFR} = 142 \cdot \min\left(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa},1\right)^{\alpha} \cdot                                     \max\left(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa},1\right)^{-1.200} \cdot                                      0.9938^{\textrm{Age}}  \cdot 1.012 \textrm{ [female]}        $$ \(\min\left(\right)\) indicates minimum \(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa}\) 1; \(\max\left(\right)\) indicates maximum \(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa}\)1. scaling parameter \(\kappa\) 0.9 males 0.7 females scaling parameter \(\alpha\) -0.302 males -0.241 females. "CKD-EPI-Scys", EGFR according Chronic Kidney Disease Epidemiology study formula (Inker): $$ \textrm{eGFR} = 133 \cdot \min\left(\frac{\textrm{Scys}}{0.8},1\right)^{-0.499} \cdot \max\left(\frac{\textrm{Scys}}{0.8},1\right)^{-1.328} \cdot 0.996^{\textrm{Age}}  \cdot 0.932 \textrm{ [female]}$$ \(\min\left(\right)\) indicates minimum \(\frac{\textrm{Scys}}{0.8}\) 1; \(\max\left(\right)\) indicates maximum \(\frac{\textrm{Scys}}{0.8}\) 1. "CKD-EPI-Scr-Scys", EGFR according Chronic Kidney Disease Epidemiology study formula (Inker): $$ \textrm{eGFR} = k \cdot l \cdot 135 \cdot \min\left(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa},1\right)^{\alpha} \cdot \max\left(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa},1\right)^{-0.601} \cdot\min\left(\frac{\textrm{Scys}}{0.8},1\right)^{-0.375} \cdot \max\left(\frac{\textrm{Scys}}{0.8},1\right)^{-0.711} \cdot 0.995^{\textrm{Age}} $$ \(\min\left(\right)\) indicates minimum \(\frac{\textrm{Scys}}{0.8}\) 1; \(\max\left(\right)\) indicates maximum \(\frac{\textrm{Scys}}{0.8}\) 1, \(\min\left(\right)\) indicates minimum \(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa}\) 1; \(\max\left(\right)\) indicates maximum \(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa}\)1. Scaling parameter k 1 males 0.969 female, scaling parameter l 1 White/Caucasian 1.08 Black/African American, scaling parameter \(\kappa\) 0.9 males 0.7 females scaling parameter \(\alpha\) -0.207 males -0.248 females. "CKD-EPI-Scr-Scys-ignore-race", EGFR according Chronic Kidney Disease Epidemiology 2021 refit without race study formula (Delgado): $$ \textrm{eGFR} = k \cdot 135 \cdot \min\left(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa},1\right)^{\alpha} \cdot \max\left(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa},1\right)^{-0.544} \cdot\min\left(\frac{\textrm{Scys}}{0.8},1\right)^{-0.323} \cdot \max\left(\frac{\textrm{Scys}}{0.8},1\right)^{-0.778} \cdot 0.9961^{\textrm{Age}} $$ \(\min\left(\right)\) indicates minimum \(\frac{\textrm{Scys}}{0.8}\) 1; \(\max\left(\right)\) indicates maximum \(\frac{\textrm{Scys}}{0.8}\) 1, \(\min\left(\right)\) indicates minimum \(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa}\) 1; \(\max\left(\right)\) indicates maximum \(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa}\)1. Scaling parameter k 1 males 0.963 female, scaling parameter \(\kappa\) 0.9 males 0.7 females scaling parameter \(\alpha\) -0.144 males -0.219 females. "CKD-EPI-Japan", EGFR Japanese adults based Japanese coefficient-modified CKD-EPI equation (Horio): $$\textrm{eGFR} =  l \cdot 141 \cdot \min\left(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa},1\right)^{\alpha} \cdot \max\left(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa},1\right)^{-1.209} \cdot 0.993^{\textrm{Age}} \cdot 1.018 \textrm{ [female]}$$ \(\min\left(\right)\) indicates minimum \(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa}\) 1; \(\max\left(\right)\) indicates maximum \(\frac{\textrm{Scr}}{\kappa}\)1. Scaling parameter l 1 White/Caucasian, 1.159 Black/African American, 0.813 Japanese, scaling parameter \(\kappa\) 0.9 males 0.7 females scaling parameter \(\alpha\) -0.411 males -0.329 females. "CKD-MDRD", EGFR according abbreviated Modification Diet Renal Disease study formula (Levey): $$\textrm{eGFR}=  186 \cdot \textrm{Scr}^{-1.154} \cdot \textrm{Age}^{-0.203} \cdot 1.212 \textrm{ [black]} \cdot 0.742 \textrm{ [female]}$$ "CKD-MDRD2", EGFR according re-expressed Modification Diet Renal Disease (MDRD) study formula (Levey2007): $$\textrm{eGFR} =   175 \cdot \textrm{Scr}^{-1.154} \cdot \textrm{Age}^{-0.203} \cdot 1.212 \textrm{ [black]} \cdot 0.742 \textrm{ [female]}$$ "Schwartz-original", EGFR children, according original Schwartz formula (Schwartz1987): $$\textrm{eGFR} = k \cdot \frac{\textrm{Height}}{\textrm{Scr}}$$ k = 0.33 pre-term infants 1 year, k = 0.45 full-term infants 1 year, k = 0.55 children 1 year 13 years, k = 0.55 girls >13 <18 years k = 0.70 boys >13 <18 years. "Schwartz-CKiD", EGFR children, according Chronic Kidney Disease Children (CKiD) revised Schwartz formula (Schwartz2012): $$\textrm{eGFR}  = 39.8 \cdot \left(\frac{\textrm{Height}}{\textrm{Scr}}\right)^{0.456}  \cdot \left(\frac{1.8}{\textrm{Scys}}\right)^{0.418}  \cdot \left(\frac{30}{\textrm{BUN}}\right)^{0.079} \cdot \left(\frac{\textrm{Height}}{1.4}\right)^{0.079}   $$ Scaling parameter k 1 males 1.076 females. "Schwartz-1B", EGFR children, according Chronic Kidney Disease Children (CKiD) 1B Schwartz formula (Schwartz2009): $$\textrm{eGFR}  = 40.7 \cdot \left(\frac{\textrm{Height}}{\textrm{Scr}}\right)^{0.64}   \cdot \left(\frac{30}{\textrm{BUN}}\right)^{0.202}  $$ "Schwartz", EGFR children, according updated ('bedside') Schwartz formula (Schwartz2009): $$\textrm{eGFR} = 0.413 \cdot \frac{\textrm{Height}}{\textrm{Scr}}$$ equation meant patients < 1 years age. "Mayo-Quadratic", EGFR according Quadratic Mayo Clinic formula (Rule). $$\textrm{eGFR} = \exp\left(1.911 + \frac{5.249}{\textrm{Scr}} - \frac{2.114}{\textrm{Scr}^2} - 0.00686 \cdot \textrm{Age} - 0.205\textrm{ [female]}\right)$$ Scr < 0.8 mg/dL, value 0.8 used equation. "Matsuo-Japan", EGFR Japanese adults, according Matsuo: $$\textrm{eGFR} =  194 \cdot \textrm{Scr}^{-1.094} \cdot \textrm{Age}^{-0.287} \cdot 0.739 \textrm{ [female]}$$ calculation methods described , reported EGFR values units "mL/minute/1.73m2". means value referenced body surface area (BSA) value 1.73m2. value provided BSA, final outcome corrected BSA value units become "mL/minute". done multiplying eGFR (referenced BSA 1.73m2) individual's BSA (users responsibility proved BSA values calculated using appropriate formula) divided 1.73. Additional information regarding can found FDA guidance document.

title: read external data with logging of results — read_data
code: # For a known filetype you can use: dat <- read_data(paste0(R.home(),"/doc/CRAN_mirrors.csv")) #> ℹ Read in /opt/R/4.5.3/lib/R/doc/CRAN_mirrors.csv which has 98 records and 9 variables  # We can use the arguments from the underlying package that does the reading xmpl <- system.file("example/Attr.Template.xlsx",package = "amp.dm") dat  <- read_data(xmpl, range="A2:B3") #> ℹ Read in /home/runner/work/_temp/Library/amp.dm/example/Attr.Template.xlsx which has 1 records and 2 variables  # In case we get a file format not directly supported by the function # we can use the manfunc to use another function sav <- system.file("examples", "iris.sav", package = "haven") dat <- read_data(sav,manfunc = "haven::read_sav") #> ℹ Read in /home/runner/work/_temp/Library/haven/examples/iris.sav which has 150 records and 5 variables  # It is also possible to write your own function that reads data # (as long as it returns a data.frame or tibble), e.g.: read_nd <- function(x) read.csv(x) |> dplyr::distinct(ID, .keep_all = TRUE) xmpl    <- system.file("example/NM.theoph.V1.csv", package = "amp.dm") dat     <- read_data(xmpl,manfunc = "read_nd") #> Error in read_data(xmpl, manfunc = "read_nd"): Manual function resulted in an error, please check syntax

text: function calculates different variables based weight height conversion kilograms

title: Calculates different weight variables — weight_height
code: weight_height(wt = NULL, ht = NULL, sex = NULL, bmi = NULL, type = "bmi")

title: Calculates different weight variables — weight_height
text: wt vector weight values, either kg lb depending type (see details) ht vector height values cm (see details) sex vector SEX values (female defined value 1) bmi vector BMI values (see details) type character type used calculations (see details)

title: Calculates different weight variables — weight_height
text: vector calculated values

title: Calculates different weight variables — weight_height
text: Currently following types defined within function:

title: Calculates different weight variables — weight_height
text: "kg-lb" : Convert units kg lb using formula $$\textrm{Weight (kg)} = \textrm{Weight (lb)} \cdot 2.20462262$$ "lb-kg" : Convert units lb kg using formula $$\textrm{Weight (lb)} = \frac{\textrm{Weight (kg)}}{2.20462262}$$

title: Calculates different weight variables — weight_height
text: "bmi" :  Calculates body mass index (BMI) using standard formula (Quetelet1842, $$\textrm{BMI} = \frac{\textrm{Weight (kg)}}{\textrm{Height (m)}^{2}}$$

title: Calculates different weight variables — weight_height
text: "bsa": Body Surface Area, according Gehan Georg, $$\textrm{BSA} = \exp{-3.751} \cdot \textrm{Height (cm)}^{0.422} \cdot \textrm{Weight (kg)}^{0.515}$$ "bsa2": Body Surface Area, according DuBois DuBois, $$\textrm{BSA} = 0.007184 \cdot \textrm{Height (cm)}^{0.725} \cdot \textrm{Weight (kg)}^{0.425}$$ "bsam": Body Surface Area, according Mosteller, $$\textrm{BSA} = \sqrt{\frac{\textrm{Weight (kg)} \cdot \textrm{Height (cm)}}{3600}}$$ "bsah": Body Surface Area, according Haycock, $$\textrm{BSA} = 0.024265 \cdot \textrm{Height (cm)}^{0.3964} \cdot \textrm{Weight (kg)}^{0.5378}$$ "bsal": Body Surface Area normal-weight obese adults 250 kg, according Livingston, $$\textrm{BSA} = 0.1173 \cdot \textrm{Weight (kg)}^{0.6466}$$

title: Calculates different weight variables — weight_height
text: "ffmj": Fat free mass, according Janmahasatian: $$\textrm{FFM}=\frac{9270 \cdot \textrm{Weight (kg)}}{k +\left(l \cdot  \textrm{BMI}\right)}$$, \(k\) 6680 males 8780 females \(l\) 216 males 244 females. "ffms": Fat free mass Indian patients, according Sinha: $$\textrm{FFM}=\frac{9270 \cdot \textrm{Weight (kg)}}{k \cdot  l \cdot \textrm{BMI}^{0.28}}$$, \(k\) 6680 males 8780 females \(l\) 0.77 males 0.70 females.

title: Calculates different weight variables — weight_height
text: "lbmb" : Calculates lean body mass (LBM), according Boers: $$\textrm{LBM} = k \cdot \textrm{Weight (kg)} + l \cdot \textrm{Height (cm)} - m $$ , \(k\) 0.407 males 0.252 females, \(l\) 0.267 males 0.473 females, \(m\) 19.2 males 48.3 females. "lbmj" : Calculates lean body mass (LBM), according James: $$\textrm{LBM} = k \cdot \textrm{Weight (kg)} - l \cdot \left(\frac{\textrm{Weight (kg)}}{\textrm{Height (cm)}}\right)^2$$, \(k\) 1.10 males 1.07 females,   \(l\) 128 males 148 females. "lbmp" : Calculates lean body mass (LBM) children 14 years, according Peters: $$\textrm{LBM} = 3.8 \cdot 0.0215 \cdot \textrm{Weight (kg)}^{0.6469} \cdot \textrm{Height (cm)}^{0.7236}$$

title: Calculates different weight variables — weight_height
text: "pnw" : Calculates Predicted Normal Weight obese patient, according Duffull: $$\textrm{PNWT} = k \cdot  \textrm{Weight (kg)} - l \cdot \textrm{Height (cm)} \cdot \textrm{BMI} - m$$, \(k\) 1.57 males 1.75 females, \(l\) 0.0183 males 0.0242 females, \(m\) 10.5 males 12.6 females.

title: Calculates different weight variables — weight_height
text: Richard Hooijmaijers

title: Calculates different weight variables — weight_height
code: tmp <- data.frame(id=1,WT=runif(3,70,120),HT=runif(3,160,220)) weight_height(wt=tmp$WT,ht=tmp$HT,type="bmi") #> [1] 29.12092 21.90432 20.51770 # example for use in dplyr tmp |> dplyr::mutate(BMI = weight_height(wt=WT,ht=HT,type="bmi")) #>   id        WT       HT      BMI #> 1  1 117.42883 200.8098 29.12092 #> 2  1  79.01694 189.9307 21.90432 #> 3  1  80.84499 198.5008 20.51770
